findings.title: Bare catch in onError callback wrapper swallows error silently
findings.description: Three interrelated error-handling issues in the extract() catch block (lines 159-165): (1) The nested catch block has no error parameter binding and no logging -- if onError throws, the failure is completely invisible. This is the same bare catch pattern found in ScreenshotPipeline.tick() and skill-loader.ts. (2) When onError is provided, errors are caught and an empty ExtractionResult is returned -- indistinguishable from 'nothing to extract', making monitoring impossible. (3) The dual-path behavior (never throws with onError, throws without onError) creates an inconsistent contract callers must know about. Even if re-throwing is undesirable, the error must at minimum be logged with context.
findings.suggestion: Bind the error and log it: `catch (callbackErr) { console.error('MemoryExtractor: onError callback threw', { original: error, callbackErr }) }`. Consider returning a Result type that distinguishes success from failure, e.g., `{ ok: false, error }` vs `{ ok: true, data: ExtractionResult }`. Document the dual contract explicitly in JSDoc.

findings.title: Bare catch in dedup loop hides embedding failures
findings.description: The catch block in the dedup loop has no error parameter binding. While the fallback behavior (include the memory rather than lose it) is a defensible choice, the error is entirely invisible -- no log, no metric, no callback to onError. A systematic embedding provider failure (wrong API key, rate limit, network down) would silently skip deduplication for all memories without any observable signal. Additionally, this is the same bare `catch {}` pattern flagged as P0 in skill-loader.ts -- error param not even bound.
findings.suggestion: Bind the error and report it: `catch (err) { this.onError?.(new Error(`Dedup embedding failed for memory: ${memory.content.slice(0, 50)}`, { cause: err })); result.push(memory) }`. This preserves the include-on-failure behavior while making failures observable.

findings.title: MemoryOrchestrator.recall hardcodes importance: 0, untested
findings.code_snippet:
return vectorResults.map(r => ({
  content: r.content,
  category: r.source,
  importance: 0,
  relevance: r.similarity,
  sourceDate: new Date(r.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 10),
}))
findings.description: The recall() method hardcodes importance to 0 for all recalled memories instead of looking up the actual importance value from DocumentStore. This means consumers of recall results (e.g., proactive triggers, persona engine) will always see importance=0, losing critical prioritization data. The test at memory-orchestrator.test.ts:128 only checks typeof relevance and never asserts the importance field, so this bug passes silently.
findings.suggestion: After vector search, look up each memory in DocumentStore by content or id to retrieve the actual importance score. Add a test that persists a memory with importance=9, recalls it, and asserts result.importance === 9. Example: expect(results[0].importance).toBe(9).

findings.title: MemoryOrchestrator and MemoryExtractor are orphan exports
findings.code_snippet:
export { MemoryOrchestrator } from './consumption/memory-orchestrator'
export type { MemoryOrchestratorOptions } from './consumption/memory-orchestrator'
...
export { MemoryExtractor } from './storage/memory-extractor'
export type { MemoryExtractorOptions } from './storage/memory-extractor'
findings.description: MemoryOrchestrator and MemoryExtractor are exported from packages/context-engine/src/index.ts but are not imported or instantiated by any code in apps/ (stage-tamagotchi, stage-web, server, etc.). No IPC handler, service registration, cron job, or event listener wires these modules to a live entry point. They exist only in tests. This is dead-on-arrival code per the orphan detection rule.
findings.suggestion: Wire MemoryOrchestrator into the Electron anima service layer (apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/) with an init call, IPC handler for recall, and a scheduled trigger (e.g., via cron-service) for daily extraction. Without this wiring, the feature cannot be exercised at runtime.

findings.title: recall() hardcodes importance: 0, losing stored importance
findings.code_snippet:
return vectorResults.map(r => ({
  content: r.content,
  category: r.source,
  importance: 0,
  relevance: r.similarity,
  sourceDate: new Date(r.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 10),
}))
findings.description: MemoryOrchestrator.recall() returns MemoryRecallResult with importance hardcoded to 0. The actual importance value (1-10) is stored in DocumentStore memory_entries but is never looked up during recall. This means all recalled memories appear equally unimportant, defeating the purpose of importance scoring. Consumers relying on importance for prioritization will get incorrect behavior.
findings.suggestion: After vector search, cross-reference with DocumentStore.getMemoryEntries() or add importance to the vector metadata. Alternatively, store importance as a field in the LanceDB record (extend ContextVector) so it is available directly from the vector search result.

findings.title: persistExtractionResults has zero error handling for multi-step I/O
findings.description: persistExtractionResults performs 5 categories of I/O operations: (1) embedding.embed per memory, (2) vectorStore.insert, (3) documentStore.insertMemoryEntry per memory, (4) documentStore.insertProfileFact per fact, (5) documentStore.upsertRelationship / insertImportantDate per item. None are wrapped in try/catch. A failure at step 2 (vectorStore.insert) leaves memories already embedded but not stored in DocumentStore, creating inconsistency between vector and document stores. A failure at step 4 aborts before relationships and dates are persisted. There is no rollback, no partial-success reporting, and no error context.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each category of persistence in its own try/catch for error isolation. At minimum, wrap the entire method body in try/catch with a descriptive error: `catch (cause) { throw new Error('Failed to persist extraction results', { cause }) }`. For production robustness, use a transaction for SQLite writes and handle vector/document stores separately.

findings.title: recall() has no error handling for embedding + vector search I/O
findings.description: recall() calls embedding.embed() and vectorStore.semanticSearch() with no try/catch. If the embedding provider is down or the query text is empty/malformed, the raw provider error propagates to the caller with no context about what operation was being attempted.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch: `catch (cause) { throw new Error(`Memory recall failed for query: "${query.text.slice(0, 80)}"`, { cause }) }`. Alternatively, return a Result type.

findings.title: No logging in MemoryExtractor or MemoryOrchestrator
findings.description: Both new modules (MemoryExtractor at 243 lines, MemoryOrchestrator at 185 lines) have zero logging. The project uses @guiiai/logg (useLogg pattern) for structured logging. Without logging: (1) LLM extraction failures are invisible in production unless onError is set, (2) dedup decisions leave no trace, (3) persistence operations have no audit trail, (4) recall queries cannot be debugged. This is a recurring finding -- task-9, task-11, and task-13 all shipped without logging.
findings.suggestion: Add useLogg('MemoryExtractor') and useLogg('MemoryOrchestrator'). Log at INFO level: extraction start/result counts, persistence counts. Log at WARN level: dedup filtered items, embedding fallbacks. Log at ERROR level: LLM failures, persistence failures.

findings.title: MemoryRecallResult.importance hardcoded to 0 in recall()
findings.code_snippet:
return vectorResults.map(r => ({
  content: r.content,
  category: r.source,
  importance: 0,
  relevance: r.similarity,
  sourceDate: new Date(r.createdAt).toISOString().slice(0, 10),
}))
findings.description: Encapsulation: 7/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 3/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.25). MemoryRecallResult declares an `importance` field typed as `number`, implying it carries meaningful information. However, the only producer (MemoryOrchestrator.recall) hardcodes it to `0` for every result. This makes the field semantically dead -- consumers cannot distinguish important from unimportant recalled memories. The type promises a value it cannot deliver.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) remove `importance` from MemoryRecallResult if it is not recoverable from the vector path, or (b) cross-reference the vector results with DocumentStore.getMemoryEntries to populate the real importance score.

findings.title: Promise.all loses entire batch + separate UUIDs prevent correlation
findings.description: Two interrelated issues in persistExtractionResults: (1) Promise.all rejects on the first failure -- if one embedding.embed() fails (rate limit, timeout), all successfully computed embeddings are discarded and no memories are persisted. This is the same Promise.all-for-independent-I/O pattern from McpHub (task-9). (2) Each memory gets two different UUIDs: one for vector store (line 125) and one for document store (line 140). There is no way to correlate a vector record with its document record, making cross-store joins, deletions, and partial-failure cleanup impossible.
findings.suggestion: Use Promise.allSettled and filter fulfilled results. Generate the UUID once per memory and use it for both stores: `const id = crypto.randomUUID()` then pass the same `id` to both vectors array and insertMemoryEntry.

findings.title: init() has no error handling for vectorStore.createTable
findings.description: init() is documented as a prerequisite that 'must be called before using recall or persistExtractionResults'. If createTable fails (e.g., disk full, permission error, LanceDB corruption), the raw error propagates with no context. Worse, the MemoryOrchestrator instance is left in an uninitialized state with no flag to prevent subsequent recall/persist calls from failing with confusing LanceDB errors about missing tables.
findings.suggestion: Wrap in try/catch with context: `catch (cause) { throw new Error('MemoryOrchestrator init failed: could not create memories table', { cause }) }`. Consider adding an `initialized` flag and checking it in recall/persistExtractionResults.

findings.title: No test for embedding failure during persistExtractionResults
findings.description: If embedding.embed() throws during persistExtractionResults(), the entire persist operation fails with an unhandled Promise rejection. There is no test verifying this error path. Memory entries that were partially persisted to DocumentStore before the embedding failure could leave the system in an inconsistent state.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with a throwing EmbeddingProvider that verifies: (1) the error is propagated or handled gracefully, (2) partial persistence state is documented or rolled back.

findings.title: L2-to-cosine comment formula notation misleads about _distance semantics
findings.code_snippet:
// L2 distance to cosine similarity: for normalized vectors, sim = 1 - (d²/2)
similarity: 1 - (c._distance / 2),
findings.description: The comment says `sim = 1 - (d^2/2)` using `d^2` notation, which implies squaring the distance value. However, LanceDB's `_distance` field already returns the *squared* L2 distance. The comment's notation suggests `_distance` is an unsquared Euclidean distance that needs squaring, which would lead a maintainer to incorrectly 'fix' the formula by squaring `_distance` first.
findings.suggestion: Rewrite the comment to: `// LanceDB _distance = squared L2; for unit vectors: cos_sim = 1 - (squared_L2 / 2)`

findings.title: Promise.all in persistExtractionResults fails entire batch on single error
findings.description: If any single embedding.embed() call fails (network timeout, rate limit, etc.), Promise.all rejects and NO memories get persisted -- even the ones that embedded successfully. For a daily batch operation processing multiple memories, losing the entire batch due to one failure is a data loss risk.
findings.suggestion: Use Promise.allSettled() and persist only the successfully embedded memories. Log warnings for failed embeddings with the memory content for retry.

findings.title: No test for embedding failure during recall()
findings.description: The recall() method calls this.embedding.embed(query.text) without any try/catch. If the embedding provider fails (network error, rate limit, API down), the error propagates unhandled to the caller. No test verifies this error path.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with a throwing EmbeddingProvider: verify that recall() either (a) propagates a typed error with context, or (b) returns an empty result set gracefully.

findings.title: Working memory is RAM-only, lost on process restart
findings.code_snippet: private workingMemory: Conversation[] = []
findings.description: MemoryOrchestrator stores Layer 1 working memory as a plain in-memory array. On Electron process crash or restart, all working memory is lost. The CLAUDE.md architecture rules state: 'critical state persisted (DB/KV/Event Store); restart-safe'. Recent conversation context is arguably critical for maintaining coherent AI character behavior across restarts.
findings.suggestion: Consider hydrating working memory from DocumentStore.getRecentConversations(capacity) on init(). This provides restart recovery with minimal overhead since the data is already persisted in SQLite.

findings.title: MemoryEntry.category is string but has documented literal values
findings.code_snippet:
/** Category (e.g., 'preference', 'event', 'habit', 'goal') */
category: string
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 3/10, Usefulness: 6/10, Enforcement: 2/10 (Aggregate: 4.0). The JSDoc documents specific valid categories ('preference', 'event', 'habit', 'goal') and the MemoryExtractor schema adds 'emotion', but the type is plain `string`. Any misspelled or invalid category silently passes type-checking. Same issue applies to ExtractedMemoryItem.category.
findings.suggestion: Define a literal union type: `export type MemoryCategory = 'preference' | 'event' | 'habit' | 'goal' | 'emotion'` and use it in both MemoryEntry.category and ExtractedMemoryItem.category.

findings.title: ExtractionInput.conversations[].role is string, not ConversationRole
findings.code_snippet: conversations: Array<{ role: string, content: string }>
findings.description: Encapsulation: 5/10, Expression: 4/10, Usefulness: 5/10, Enforcement: 3/10 (Aggregate: 4.25). ConversationRole is already defined as `'user' | 'assistant' | 'system'` in storage/types.ts. ExtractionInput.conversations uses an inline anonymous type with `role: string` instead of reusing ConversationRole, widening the type unnecessarily.
findings.suggestion: Change the type to: `conversations: Array<{ role: ConversationRole, content: string }>` and import ConversationRole from storage/types.

findings.title: hasNearDeadlineTodos() checks uncompleted todos, not deadlines
findings.description: The method is named hasNearDeadlineTodos() suggesting it checks for approaching deadlines, but the Todo type has no dueDate/deadline field. The implementation simply checks if any uncompleted todo exists. This is semantically misleading.
findings.suggestion: Rename to hasUncompletedTodos() to match actual behavior, or add a dueDate field to the Todo type and implement actual deadline proximity checking.

findings.title: hasNearDeadlineTodos name/doc implies deadline proximity but checks any incomplete
findings.description: The method is named `hasNearDeadlineTodos` which implies it checks for todos that are near their deadline, but the implementation simply returns true if any todo is incomplete. The method name functions as implicit documentation that contradicts the code.
findings.suggestion: Either rename to `hasIncompleteTodos()` to match actual behavior, or implement actual deadline proximity logic.

findings.title: ImportantDate.dateType is string but has documented literal values
findings.code_snippet:
/** Category of date (e.g., 'birthday', 'anniversary', 'deadline') */
dateType: string
findings.description: Same primitive obsession pattern as MemoryEntry.category. The JSDoc documents known dateType values but TypeScript type is unconstrained `string`. Invalid dateType values would silently pass through and persist to SQLite.
findings.suggestion: Define `export type DateType = 'birthday' | 'anniversary' | 'deadline'` and use it for both ImportantDate.dateType and ExtractedImportantDate.dateType.

findings.title: MemoryOrchestrator JSDoc references LanceDB by name -- implementation coupling
findings.code_snippet:
/**
 * Three-layer memory orchestrator:
 *   Layer 1: Working Memory (RAM)
 *   Layer 2: Real-time Memory (LanceDB)
 *   Layer 3: Structured Memory (SQLite)
 */
findings.description: The class JSDoc names specific storage technologies (LanceDB, SQLite) but the class depends on VectorStore and DocumentStore abstractions. If implementations change, this comment will become stale and misleading.
findings.suggestion: Replace technology names with abstraction names: `Layer 2: Real-time Memory (VectorStore)` and `Layer 3: Structured Memory (DocumentStore)`.

findings.title: persistExtractionResults is 68 lines with 4 sequential blocks
findings.description: This method handles persistence for 4 different entity types (memories, profileFacts, relationships, importantDates) in a single function spanning 68 lines. While each block is straightforward, the function exceeds the 50-line threshold and could be decomposed.
findings.suggestion: Extract focused helper methods: persistMemories, persistProfileFacts, persistRelationships, persistImportantDates. This also fixes the dual-UUID issue if UUIDs are generated and shared within persistMemories.

findings.title: No test for empty conversations input to MemoryExtractor
findings.description: All MemoryExtractor tests use makeExtractionInput() which always provides 3 conversations, 2 activities, and 1 todo. No test exercises the empty-input boundary. The buildUserPrompt method has conditional blocks for empty inputs that are never exercised by tests.
findings.suggestion: Add tests for boundary inputs: (1) empty conversations array, (2) empty activities, (3) all empty.

findings.title: Relationship.relationshipType is unconstrained string
findings.code_snippet: relationshipType: string
findings.description: Unlike dateType and category which at least have JSDoc hints, relationshipType has no documented valid values at all. Different LLM runs could produce 'friend', 'Friend', 'close friend' for semantically similar relationships, making queries and dedup unreliable.
findings.suggestion: Define a literal union: `export type RelationshipType = 'family' | 'friend' | 'colleague' | 'romantic' | 'acquaintance' | 'other'` and add normalization in MemoryExtractor validation.

findings.title: Silent catch in dedup swallows embedding errors without logging
findings.description: When embedding fails during dedup, the error is silently swallowed. The comment explains the rationale (prefer including over losing), which is a reasonable fallback strategy. However, without logging, repeated embedding failures will be invisible. If the embedding service is down, every memory will skip dedup silently, potentially causing massive duplication.
findings.suggestion: Log a warning with the error and memory content. The fallback behavior (include the memory) is correct, but observability is missing.

findings.title: No input validation on DocumentStore memory table methods
findings.description: All new DocumentStore methods accept values without validating business constraints: confidence must be 0-1, importance must be 1-10, date format should be MM-DD or YYYY-MM-DD. This creates a defense-in-depth gap.
findings.suggestion: Add lightweight validation at the DocumentStore boundary: throw on confidence < 0 or > 1, importance < 1 or > 10, empty strings for required fields.

findings.title: L2-to-cosine conversion assumes normalized vectors without validation
findings.code_snippet:
// L2 distance to cosine similarity: for normalized vectors, sim = 1 - (d²/2)
similarity: 1 - (c._distance / 2),
findings.description: The semanticSearch method converts L2 distance to cosine similarity assuming normalized vectors and squared L2 distance from LanceDB. Neither assumption is validated: non-normalized vectors produce similarity outside [0,1], and a LanceDB metric change would silently break the conversion.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime assertion: if similarity < 0 or > 1, log a warning. Consider clamping: Math.max(0, Math.min(1, 1 - (c._distance / 2))).

findings.title: New storage interfaces lack readonly modifiers on all fields
findings.description: UserProfileFact, Relationship, ImportantDate, and MemoryEntry are all new storage types representing persisted records that should be immutable. All fields are mutable, allowing consumers to accidentally modify records without persisting changes.
findings.suggestion: Add `readonly` to all fields on these record interfaces, or wrap them with `Readonly<T>` at the export site.

findings.title: MemoryOrchestrator.recall threshold parameter never tested
findings.description: The recall() method accepts an optional threshold parameter forwarded to vectorStore.semanticSearch(). While semanticSearch threshold is tested in vector-store-semantic.test.ts, the orchestrator's threshold forwarding is never tested. A wiring bug would go undetected.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that passes a high threshold to recall() and verifies that low-similarity results are filtered out.

findings.title: cosineSimilarity imported from smart-todo is a shared utility
findings.code_snippet: import { cosineSimilarity } from '../consumption/smart-todo'
findings.description: cosineSimilarity is a pure math utility now used by both smart-todo.ts and memory-extractor.ts. Importing from a domain-specific module creates a misleading dependency.
findings.suggestion: Extract to packages/context-engine/src/utils/math.ts and import from there in both files.

findings.title: New public methods in DocumentStore lack JSDoc despite project convention
findings.description: The 11 new public methods all lack JSDoc comments. The existing methods also lack JSDoc (internally consistent), but CLAUDE.md convention requires JSDoc on all exported interfaces and functions.
findings.suggestion: Add brief JSDoc to each new public method, at minimum describing parameters and return values for methods with non-obvious behavior.

findings.title: insertImportantDate allows duplicate entries for same event
findings.description: Unlike upsertRelationship (which uses ON CONFLICT), insertImportantDate uses plain INSERT. If the LLM extracts the same date multiple times across runs, duplicate rows accumulate, leading to unbounded growth.
findings.suggestion: Add a UNIQUE constraint on (date, label) or (date, date_type, label) and use ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE.

findings.title: Magic number 3 in semanticSearch candidate multiplier
findings.code_snippet: const candidates = await this.search(tableName, queryVector, topK * 3)
findings.description: The multiplier 3 in `topK * 3` is a magic number representing the over-fetch ratio for threshold filtering but is not documented as a named constant.
findings.suggestion: Extract to: `const CANDIDATE_OVERFETCH_RATIO = 3`

findings.title: Section divider comments in DocumentStore restate class structure
findings.description: Section dividers merely restate what method names already convey. In a 266-line file, IDE navigation provides this structure.
findings.suggestion: Consider removing section dividers or rely on IDE symbol navigation.

findings.title: StubEmbeddingProvider duplicated across 3 test files
findings.description: The exact same StubEmbeddingProvider class is defined in memory-extractor.test.ts, memory-integration.test.ts, and memory-orchestrator.test.ts. Similarly, StubLlmProvider is duplicated. This violates DRY.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared test doubles into __tests__/fixtures/stub-providers.ts and import in each test file.

findings.title: No Valibot schemas at memory extraction LLM boundary
findings.description: The project standard is Valibot for schema validation. Manual type guards are functional but more error-prone and harder to keep in sync with TypeScript interfaces. Current manual guards work correctly and follow the SmartTip gold-standard pattern. Flagged as P3 informational.
findings.suggestion: Consider migrating to Valibot schemas for ExtractionResult validation in a future iteration.

findings.title: Redundant double cast in importantDates validation
findings.description: After isValidImportantDate has already validated that d has date, dateType, and label as strings, the map casts through unknown back to Record and re-casts each field. The type guard already narrows the type, making most of this redundant.
findings.suggestion: Trust the type guard and access fields directly, or expand the guard to cover description.

findings.title: Inline comment in catch block states the obvious
findings.code_snippet: catch { /* onError callback itself failed */ }
findings.description: The comment explains what is already obvious from the surrounding code structure. Any developer reading this code can deduce that the catch is guarding against the callback throwing.
findings.suggestion: Remove or make actionable: `/* Swallow to prevent callback errors from masking the original extraction failure */`.

positive_observations: LLM output is validated with runtime type guards (validateExtractionResult + individual isValid* functions) before use -- a clear improvement over task-13 which trusted LLM output without validation
Test suite uses real SQLite (better-sqlite3 with temp dirs) and real LanceDB for storage tests -- correct testing pattern per CLAUDE.md, no InMemoryRepository anti-pattern
Test Doubles for LLM and Embedding APIs are properly documented with '// Test Double rationale:' comments explaining why the external service is stubbed
Full E2E pipeline test (memory-integration.test.ts) proves extract -> persist -> recall data flow across all three layers with real storage
DocumentStore uses parameterized queries throughout -- no SQL injection risk in the new methods
MemoryExtractor gracefully degrades on embedding failure during dedup (includes memory rather than losing it)
Clean three-layer architecture (working memory / vector search / structured storage) with clear separation of concerns
Excellent JSDoc on all interfaces in storage/types.ts with concrete examples (e.g., 'MM-DD for recurring or YYYY-MM-DD for one-time')
The MemoryExtractor class-level JSDoc includes a clear flow diagram (input -> prompt -> LLM -> validate -> filter -> dedup -> result)
VectorStore uses a static factory method (create) to handle async initialization, avoiding constructor async antipattern
DocumentStore constructor properly cleans up (db.close()) in catch block on init failure -- good resource management pattern
VectorStore.getDb() guard prevents use-after-close with a clear error message
VectorStore.deleteById escapes single quotes in the ID string to prevent LanceDB filter injection
MemoryOrchestrator.getWorkingMemory() returns a spread copy as readonly Conversation[] -- good immutability pattern
VectorSource literal union was properly extended with 'memory', 'preference', 'relationship' rather than widening to string
All functions in the changed files are short and focused with clear single responsibilities (except persistExtractionResults)
Consistent use of early returns and guard clauses throughout
No vi.fn(), vi.mock(), vi.spyOn(), jest.fn(), jest.mock(), InMemoryRepository, MockXxx, FakeXxx, or it.skip patterns found in test files
MemoryExtractor.extract() wraps errors with new Error(msg, { cause }) providing good error chain context
